one_line_takeaway: SafeClaw-R adds a structural graph invariant that forces every functional action in an OpenClaw-style multi-agent assistant through a paired enforcement node before execution, and an automated pipeline to generate those enforcement ('safe skill') counterparts, reaching 95.2% accuracy on Google Workspace risk scenarios (vs 61.6% regex), 97.8% malicious third-party skill detection, and 100% accuracy on a 2,020-case adversarial code-execution benchmark, while surfacing concrete false-positive/false-negative patterns (bulk-email thresholding, noun- vs verb-based rule matching, over-use of REVIEW) as its own documented weaknesses.
key_finding: On Google Workspace (2,000+ test cases across 20 scenarios, Table 3), SafeClaw-R achieves 95.2% overall accuracy against ground-truth BLOCK/REVIEW/PASS labels, versus 61.6% for the regex baseline (a 33.6 percentage-point improvement); 19 of 20 scenarios exceed 90% accuracy. The weakest scenario is 1D 'email bombing' at 78.8% (vs. regex 45.5%), attributed to an underspecified BLOCK/REVIEW threshold on bulk recipient count. Regex is competitive only on CLI-structured inputs (e.g., 3A: 82%, 7B: 76%) but collapses on free-form natural-language/social-engineering cases (e.g., 2C: 37%, 2D: 43%, 5A: 52%). On the third-party MaliciousAgentSkills benchmark, SafeClaw-R (called 'Skill-guard' in this section) achieves 97.8% average detection across 49 confirmed malicious skills / 12 threat-pattern categories (Table 4), with no false positives observed within the evaluated malicious set (note: the dataset contains no benign skills, so a true FP rate over benign skills cannot be computed), and identifies a specific false negative case (bsa-brainstorm) involving a subtle 'instruction override via persona adoption' pattern that motivated adding a 'transparency is not safety' rule. On the Safe-Exec code-execution benchmark (Table 6), SafeClaw-R achieves 100% accuracy on all 2,020 cases (1,010 malicious M-Series mutants correctly BLOCKed, 1,010 benign B-Series mutants correctly PASSed), including robustness across all 9 cosmetic mutation operators, indicating detection relies on semantic data flow (e.g., core attack tokens like socket.gethostbyname, authorized_keys, CGEventTap) rather than surface pattern matching. On usability (RQ2), Google Workspace shows a 3.4% false-positive rate (7 FPs out of 205 negative cases), traced to rules matching on domain nouns rather than action directionality (e.g., blocking local-only contact exports, conflating additive vs. destructive sheet edits, misclassifying draft-deletion as risky, blocking benign re-auth flows); code execution shows zero false positives on the B-Series. On third-party skills, the paper notes SafeClaw-R skews toward REVIEW over hard BLOCK for many malicious skills, a conservative-but-friction-inducing tendency attributed to coarse decision granularity (lack of LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH/CRITICAL stratification).
core_contribution: SafeClaw-R is a runtime safety-enforcement framework for LLM-based multi-agent personal assistants built on top of OpenClaw. Its own contribution (distinct from the cited related work in Section 6) is threefold: (1) a structural invariant over the multi-agent execution graph requiring that every functional node n_f in the graph G=(N,E), N=N_f∪N_e, be preceded by a corresponding enforcement node n_e such that (n_e, n_f) ∈ E and n_f executes only if n_e authorizes it -- implemented as a system-level override that always schedules enforcement agents before functional agents when multiple skills are triggered; (2) a (Trigger, Task, Resources) abstraction for specifying both functional skills and enforcement nodes, with a defined resource vocabulary (stop, user_confirm, log_event, quarantine, modify_output, failure_memory, CLI) for mediating actions (Table 2); and (3) the 'Safe Skill Factory,' an automated six-phase closed-loop meta-skill pipeline (threat modeling & action mapping -> SKILL.md spec writing -> test case generation -> benchmark evaluation -> FP/FN root-cause analysis -> iterative spec refinement) that synthesizes a 1-to-1 'safe counterpart' skill for each existing OpenClaw skill, feeding a proposed community repository called SafeSkillHub. The paper also reports an original preliminary risk analysis of OpenClaw's 55 built-in skills across four dimensions (reversibility, blast radius, externality, sensitivity), finding 36.4% rated high or critical risk (Table 1).
threat_model: The paper targets locally-deployed, skill/tool-based LLM multi-agent personal assistants (concretely OpenClaw) that have broad access to personal cloud services (Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Contacts), local bash execution, and third-party/community-contributed skills. Three risk categories are named directly from the authors' own risk analysis: R1 irreversible actions (e.g., deletion, external communication), R2 high-blast-radius operations affecting shared/external resources, and R3 sensitive-data interactions (credential/PII exposure). Concrete threat classes evaluated are (a) prompt injection embedded in emails/documents that hijacks agent reasoning, (b) skill poisoning, where a third-party skill (e.g., a 'calculator' skill in Figure 6) hides malicious code such as a reverse-shell payload behind a benign natural-language description, and (c) malicious or covert code instructions executed via the agent's bash tool, including credential exfiltration, DNS hijacking, keylogging via CGEventTap, and semantic-preserving code mutations meant to evade detection. The paper also documents a real-world incident (cited from a Business Insider report, not original to this paper) in which an OpenClaw agent misinterpreted a command and deleted an entire email history despite an explicit user stop instruction, used to motivate the need for pre-execution (not post-hoc) mediation.
limitations: Limitations stated or clearly evidenced in the paper's own text: (1) the 1D email-bombing scenario (78.8%) reveals an underspecified BLOCK/REVIEW threshold that both over-blocks legitimate bulk sends and is susceptible to social-engineering framing for large sends; (2) the Google Workspace false-positive analysis (3.4%, 7/205) identifies a systemic root cause -- rules keyed on domain nouns rather than verb/intent directionality -- across four concrete failure patterns (contact export destination, sheet edit type, draft deletion intent, re-auth flow); (3) the third-party skill evaluation cannot measure a true false-positive rate because the MaliciousAgentSkills dataset the authors use contains no benign skills, so precision/recall trade-offs are only partially characterized, which the authors flag as future work; (4) the paper explicitly notes SafeClaw-R over-relies on the REVIEW verdict for malicious skills rather than confident BLOCK decisions, a granularity limitation; (5) the Safe-Exec benchmark's own authors note coverage gaps they leave to future work: long scripts, multi-file exploits, dynamic attribute access, sandbox-aware payloads, and polyglot files, plus that 'semantic evasion remains an untested frontier' (e.g., subprocess substitution, polyglot encoding) that could potentially bypass the invariant; (6) the paper is explicitly framed as 'Under Submission' with an anonymized SafeSkillHub reference, i.e., not yet peer-reviewed; (7) evaluation is conducted entirely on benchmarks built/curated by the authors (except the third-party skill dataset), so real-world deployment overhead, latency, and the true efficiency-safety tradeoff of the proposed hybrid regex+LLM division of labor (Section 4.4) is discussed only qualitatively, not measured.

datasets_used: Author-constructed Google Workspace benchmark: 20 risk scenarios across 7 service domains (Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Contacts, Sheets/Docs, Cross-service, Auth/Token), ~100 test cases per scenario across 4 categories (direct/CLI, natural-language, adversarial/obfuscated, true negatives)

MaliciousAgentSkills dataset (Liu et al., cited as reference [16]): 157 confirmed malicious skills / 632 annotated vulnerabilities in the source dataset; authors used 49 malicious skills after filtering invalidated links, spanning 12 threat-pattern categories

Author-constructed 'Safe-Exec' benchmark: 20 hand-authored Python seed scripts (10 benign B-Series, 10 malicious M-Series) expanded with 2,000 semantic-preserving cosmetic mutations (9 mutation operator types) to 2,020 total test cases
Original preliminary risk analysis of OpenClaw's 55 built-in skills (not a downstream evaluation benchmark, but an authors' own dataset supporting Table 1)
quantitative_results: Preliminary risk analysis: 36.4% of OpenClaw's 55 built-in skills rated high or critical risk (10.9% critical, 25.5% high, 41.8% medium, 21.8% low; Table 1)
Google Workspace overall accuracy: SafeClaw-R 95.2% vs Regex baseline 61.6% (33.6 percentage-point gap), across 20 scenarios; 19/20 scenarios exceed 90% accuracy for SafeClaw-R
Weakest Google Workspace scenario: 1D email bombing, SafeClaw-R 78.8% vs Regex 45.5%

Strongest examples: several critical scenarios (6B persistence, 7B OAuth token exfiltration, 3A public Drive exposure, 6A exfiltrate chain) at 100.0% for SafeClaw-R vs 65.3%/75.6%/82.0%/73.7% for Regex respectively

Third-party malicious skill detection: 97.8% average detection rate across 49 confirmed malicious skills / 12 threat patterns (Table 4), with block-rate per pattern varying widely (e.g., Unpinned Dependencies 0% block rate despite 100% 'detect', i.e. classified as REVIEW not BLOCK; Data Exfiltration 75% block rate)

Code execution: 100% accuracy on Safe-Exec benchmark, 1,010/1,010 malicious cases correctly BLOCKed, 1,010/1,010 benign cases correctly PASSed (Table 6), robust across all 9 cosmetic mutation operators
Usability: Google Workspace false-positive rate 3.4% (7 FPs / 205 negative cases); code execution 0 false positives on B-Series (1,010 benign mutants)
baselines_compared: Regex-based detection baseline (deterministic pattern-matching rules), compared against SafeClaw-R across all 20 Google Workspace risk scenarios in Table 3 -- the paper's only quantitative baseline comparison; no other prior systems (e.g., GuardAgent, ShieldAgent, NeMo Guardrails, AgentDoG, mentioned only in Related Work) are empirically benchmarked against SafeClaw-R in this paper
